name: Kapa'a Quarry (private area, obtain permission first)
short_description: Private quarry site near East Honolulu featuring volcanic rock formations with feldspar and olivine crystals. Permission required before collecting.
description:
Kapa'a Quarry is a privately owned quarry located on the eastern side of Oahu near East Honolulu. The site sits within the volcanic geology that characterizes the Hawaiian Islands, where ancient basaltic lava flows have created opportunities to study and collect volcanic minerals. The quarry exposes fresh basalt outcrops containing visible feldspar crystals and olivine grains typical of Hawaiian volcanism.

The terrain here is relatively gentle with low elevation (236 feet), making access physically manageable for most visitors. However, this is private property and collectors must obtain explicit permission from the property owners before entering or collecting. The quarry environment presents typical volcanic rock formations with sharp edges and loose material common to mining and excavation sites.

Visitors interested in Hawaiian geology and volcanic minerals will find Kapa'a Quarry scientifically interesting, but the private ownership status means access is limited and must be arranged in advance. The site offers a closer look at the mineral composition of Oahu's volcanic bedrock compared to public beach and stream collecting areas.
history: Kapa'a Quarry sits within the Honolulu Volcanic Series, formed during Pleistocene volcanic activity that built much of eastern Oahu. The basaltic rocks exposed here crystallized from lava flows approximately 3.7 to 3.3 million years ago, with olivine and feldspar minerals forming as the magma cooled, creating the mineral assemblages collectors seek today.
tips: Always contact the quarry owners or managers before visiting - private property access is strictly controlled. Bring sturdy closed-toe boots and gloves since basalt is extremely sharp and cutting; the exposed rock faces and loose material create hazardous footing. Look for olivine crystals (green, translucent grains) and feldspar (white to cream-colored crystals) in the fresh basalt faces; these minerals are most visible in newly exposed surfaces rather than weathered rocks. Work slowly and carefully around vertical or undercut rock faces typical of quarry sites.
terrain_notes: Elevation 236 feet with gentle slope characteristic of eastern Oahu foothills. Terrain is rocky volcanic bedrock with sharp basalt fragments, loose rubble from quarry operations, and exposed rock faces. Walking surfaces are uneven and treacherous. No established trails or smooth paths. Active or inactive quarry environment with potential unstable zones. Footing requires constant attention and sturdy footwear.
best_season: Year-round, with preference for November to March when temperatures are cooler (70-80 degrees F) and occasional rain keeps dust levels lower. Summer months (June-August) can be hot and dry, but quarry sites offer minimal shade. Winter brings more humid conditions and occasional heavy rain typical of windward Oahu, but this doesn't significantly impact quarry access.
written_directions: From East Honolulu, take Kalanianaole Highway (HI-72) east toward Waimanalo. Kapa'a Quarry is located inland from the coastal road in the foothills area south of Kailua. Exact directions require prior contact with property owners, as the quarry is private land without public signage or marked access points. GPS coordinates (21.389346, -157.774851) will guide you to the general location, but you must obtain permission and specific access instructions from the property management before visiting.
road_conditions: Approach via paved highway (Kalanianaole Highway). Final access to quarry likely requires unpaved road or private driveway; conditions unknown without property owner contact.
parking_notes: No public parking designated. Parking arrangement must be made with property owners during permission request.
collection_rules: This is private property. Permission from property owners or site managers is mandatory before entering or collecting any material. No commercial collection permitted. Respect all posted signs and boundaries. Collect only in areas explicitly authorized by property management. Remove all trash and respect the site. Follow all safety protocols established by the quarry operators.
rules:
Permission from property owners required before access
No collection without explicit authorization
No commercial collection
Respect all posted signs and boundaries
Follow quarry safety protocols
Remove all trash
Closed-toe shoes and protective gear required
nearest_services: Kailua town, approximately 4 miles west, offers gas, restaurants, and supplies. East Honolulu proper has limited services; Waimanalo (5 miles east) has a few shops.
accessibility_notes: Rough quarry terrain with sharp basalt fragments, loose rock, and unstable surfaces. Uneven footing throughout. Not wheelchair accessible. Not suitable for limited mobility. Steep or vertical rock faces present. Requires sturdy closed-toe boots, gloves, and protective gear. Hazardous for young children or inexperienced hikers. Only experienced rockhounders comfortable with industrial/mining environments should attempt access.

faq.question: Can I collect basalt and feldspar at Kapa'a Quarry without permission?
faq.answer: No. Kapa'a Quarry is private property, and permission from the owners or site managers is mandatory before entering or collecting any material. There is no public access to this quarry. Contact the property owners in advance to request collecting permission and obtain specific access instructions.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find rockhounding at Kapa'a Quarry near Honolulu?
faq.answer: Kapa'a Quarry exposes basaltic volcanic rock containing feldspar crystals (white to cream-colored) and olivine grains (green, translucent). These minerals crystallized from ancient Hawaiian lava flows and are visible in the fresh basalt exposures throughout the quarry. The site offers a close look at the mineral composition of Oahu's volcanic bedrock.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Kapa'a Quarry in Hawaii?
faq.answer: Year-round collecting is possible, but November through March is preferred when temperatures are cooler (70-80 degrees F) and humidity is lower. Summer months (June-August) can be hot and dry with minimal shade, making physical work in the quarry more taxing. Winter brings occasional rain typical of windward Oahu, but this does not significantly impact quarry access.

faq.question: Is Kapa'a Quarry suitable for beginner rockhounders or families?
faq.answer: No. Kapa'a Quarry is a private industrial quarry with sharp basalt, loose rock, and unstable surfaces. It is not suitable for beginners, children, or families. Only experienced rockhounders comfortable with hazardous mining environments and equipped with protective gear (sturdy boots, gloves, eye protection) should attempt access. Permission is required and access is strictly controlled.

faq.question: What tools and protective gear do I need for rockhounding at Kapa'a Quarry?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy closed-toe boots (basalt is extremely sharp), heavy gloves, eye protection, and a hard hat if entering quarry areas. Pack hand tools such as a geology hammer and chisel for specimen collection. The sharp volcanic rock cuts easily, and loose material creates hazardous footing, so protective gear is essential. Also bring water, sunscreen, and a first aid kit.

faq.question: How far is Kapa'a Quarry from Honolulu and what are the driving directions?
faq.answer: Kapa'a Quarry is approximately 4 miles east of Kailua town on eastern Oahu. From East Honolulu, take Kalanianaole Highway (HI-72) east toward Waimanalo. The quarry is located inland in the foothills. Exact directions and final access require prior contact with property owners at the private site, and you must obtain permission before visiting.

meta_description: Private quarry near Honolulu for basalt, feldspar, and olivine. Permission required. Experienced rockhounders only. East Oahu geology.
